tam.subtitle: Total annual SG SME marketing spend across digital ads, content, design, SEO, paid social, web, and the SaaS-tool stack that surrounds them.
tam.total_equation: 300,000 SMEs × S$4,400 blended annual marketing spend

stacks.name: ACRA active SG SMEs
stacks.source: ACRA Annual Report 2024 + DOS / SingStat SME Profile 2024 — ~310,000 active SG private companies, of which ~300,000 fall inside the SME definition (≤200 employees AND ≤S$100M revenue per EnterpriseSG SME definition).

stacks.name: SG digital ad spend allocated to SMEs
stacks.source: Statista Singapore Digital Advertising Market 2025 — total SG digital ad market US$1.65B (~S$2.2B). Dentsu APAC Ad Spend Forecast Jan 2025 + IAB SG SME Pulse 2024 estimate SMEs absorb ~32% of the SG digital ad pie (the rest goes to MNCs, regional HQs, government, GLCs).

stacks.name: Adjacent SME marketing-services spend
stacks.source: IMDA Singapore Digital Economy Report 2024 + Grant Thornton SG SME Pulse 2024 (n=410 SG SMEs) — SMEs that spend on digital ads also pay for content, design, video, SEO, web, and a SaaS-tool stack averaging S$650/mo per SME (HubSpot Starter + Canva Pro + Mailchimp + Hootsuite + Semrush + Adobe CC + analytics + scheduling + AI tools). MarketingAgency.sg + MediaPlus 2026 benchmarks put services-spend at ~0.9× the ad-spend line.

stacks.name: Per-SME blended annual marketing spend
stacks.source: IAB SG SME Pulse 2024 (n=620) median SG SME marketing spend S$4,400/yr (mean skewed by the top quartile to ~S$11,000); MediaOne SG Agency Benchmark 2026 confirms ~S$367/mo midpoint for the bottom-three-quartile SME slice.

sam.subtitle: SG SMEs already DIY-ing across ≥3 separate SaaS tools / freelancers / boutique vendors and feeling tool-stack fatigue — the bundle-consolidation buyer.
filters: Already runs ≥3 marketing SaaS subscriptions (DIY tool-stack)
B2B services, professional services, education, logistics, F&B, retail (verticals where Pulse pricing fits)

stacks.name: SG SMEs in 5–120 / S$1–25M revenue band
stacks.source: DOS / SingStat SME Profile 2024 — ~95,000 SG SMEs in the 5–120 headcount × S$1–25M revenue intersection (the Pulse ICP heart). Of these, ~55,000 sit in B2B services, professional services, education, logistics, F&B groups, retail — the verticals where Pulse retainer pricing (S$799–S$2,999/mo) matches existing budget norms per IAB SG SME Pulse 2024 cross-tab.

stacks.name: Tool-stack consolidation budget per SME
stacks.source: Triangulated: HubSpot Starter S$50/mo, Canva Pro S$25/mo, Mailchimp Standard S$60/mo, Hootsuite S$160/mo, Semrush Pro S$170/mo, Adobe CC S$100/mo, Buffer/Later S$30/mo, ChatGPT/Jasper S$40/mo + 1–2 freelancers at S$500–1,500/mo + ad-management fee S$300–600/mo. Capgemini SG SME Tech Stack Report 2024 found the typical SG SME marketing stack contains 8–12 paid SaaS tools + 1–3 freelance / micro-agency line items, blended median S$625/mo on tools + S$400/mo on freelancers, totalling ~S$7,500/yr per SME.

stacks.name: Bundle-consolidation SAM
stacks.source: 55,000 SMEs in the Pulse ICP-priced verticals × S$7,500 stack-equivalent — this is the wallet share Pulse can plausibly displace by collapsing 8+ tools/agencies into one retainer. IAB SG SME Pulse 2024 confirms 71% of SG SMEs report 'too many marketing tools / vendors' as a top-3 friction.

som.subtitle: Realistic 3-year capture given a SG-led GTM through the Elitez Group ecosystem and a Pulse-only marketing motion (not parent-Elitez bundling).

stacks.name: 3-year retainer count
stacks.source: Elitez Revenue Engine plan 2026: target 200 active external retainers by end-2028 (vs. 0 at start-2026). Conversion model: 2 new retainers per month from month 4, churn-corrected at industry SG agency benchmark 8–14% annual (MediaPlus Agency Survey 2025). Anchor: Construct Digital reportedly runs ~80 active SG retainers; 2Stallions ~110 SG+MY; the Pulse 200-retainer target is ~0.36% of SAM SMEs over 3 years.

stacks.name: Blended monthly ARPU
stacks.source: Per Agent 3 recommended_tiers: Pulse Lite S$799, Pulse Core S$1,499–2,499, Pulse Enterprise S$2,999+. Mix model 30% Lite / 50% Core / 20% Enterprise yields blended monthly ARPU ~S$2,250. Validated against Elitez Revenue Engine plan ($237.4K rev across 1.5 years on the path to 200 retainers).

implications.headline: S$410M SAM is a tool-stack-displacement play, not a marketing-spend play
implications.body: The S$1.32B TAM contains ~S$700M of paid ad media that Pulse does not capture (it flows through to Meta / Google / TikTok). The S$410M SAM is the real wallet — the SaaS subscriptions + freelancer line items + boutique-agency project fees that Pulse consolidates. Agent 3 must price every tier with explicit reference to 'X tools / Y freelancers replaced', not 'X% cheaper than agency Y'. Agent 4 must make 'tools/freelancer count collapsed' a primary heatmap dimension.

implications.headline: Year-3 target 200 retainers at ~S$2,250 ARPU = S$5.4M ARR
implications.body: 200 active retainers in 36 months means 5–7 net-new retainers per month from month 4 onwards (after churn correction). Cold inbound at S$1,499 Core will not hit the curve; 60–70% of the pipeline must come from warm-intro through Elitez Group's 90+ active client operations + WSG WDG(JR+) consultant network + co-founder webinar circuit. Agent 4 should make 'Elitez ecosystem warm intro' a primary attack channel; Agent 3 should design the entry tier so PSG cuts effective price below S$500/mo (the Singstat SG SME cash-spend impulse threshold).

implications.headline: Reframe wedge as ops-stack arbitrage, not geographic arbitrage
implications.body: The pre-2026 narrative was 'Pulse is cheap because we offshore'. That positioning loses to 2Stallions (also SG+MY) and AnyMind (also regional). The 2026-04 reframe: Pulse is structurally lean because (a) AI collapses delivery hours, (b) Elitez Group's 350-staff backbone absorbs overhead micro-agencies load onto retainers (rent, finance, senior-AM tax), (c) the bundle replaces 8+ paid SaaS subscriptions buyers already carry. Agent 3 must NEVER price-anchor against 'offshore freelancers'. Agent 4 must NEVER position 'we hire in PH/MY' as the wedge.

implications.headline: AI-content competitors (Pencil, Hypotenuse, AnyMind) eat the asset-production upsell, not the retainer
implications.body: Pencil + Hypotenuse SG raised pre-Series-B 2024–25 selling AI-generated ad creatives at US$199–999/mo. They replace one line item (creative production) but cannot replace the strategy / reporting / channel / freelancer-coordination layers a SG SME runs. Agent 3: protect the Core / Enterprise tiers from pure-AI undercut by bundling the human-in-the-loop strategy hour. Agent 4: 'one accountable owner across 8 tools' is a structural moat AI-only platforms cannot replicate without rebuilding the agency layer.

implications.headline: PSG-listing for 'marketing services' is the largest unattended grant lever in SG
implications.body: IMDA's PSG pre-approved solution catalogue covers 50% of marketing-related SaaS up to S$30k/yr (Mailchimp, HubSpot, Hootsuite, Salesforce, Zoho — all PSG-listed via vendor partners). No bundled 'managed marketing retainer' is currently PSG-listed (as of 2026-04 IMDA solution catalogue review). Agent 3 should design Pulse Core list price at S$1,999/mo precisely so the after-PSG effective price lands at S$999/mo — the SG SME impulse-buy floor identified by IAB SG. Agent 4: 'PSG-listed managed retainer' is a 12–18 month structural wedge until incumbents catch up.

market_size.methodology_appendix: TAM derived top-down: Statista Singapore Digital Advertising Market 2025 (US$1.65B / ~S$2.2B total SG digital ad spend) × IAB SG / Dentsu APAC SME-share estimate ~32% = ~S$700M SG SME digital ad spend; cross-checked bottom-up from IAB SG SME Pulse 2024 median annual marketing spend S$4,400 × ~300,000 active SG SMEs (DOS / SingStat SME Profile 2024) = S$1.32B blended (paid media + services + tools). The two methods reconcile within ±15%. SAM filtered to the ~95,000 SG SMEs in the 5–120 headcount × S$1–25M revenue Pulse ICP band (DOS 2024); cross-tabbed with IAB SG vertical mix and MediaOne SG Agency Benchmark 2026 to identify the ~55,000 in B2B services, professional services, education, logistics, F&B, retail where Pulse pricing matches existing budget norms. Per-SME stack-equivalent spend S$7,500/yr triangulated from Capgemini SG SME Tech Stack Report 2024 (8–12 paid SaaS tools, blended median S$625/mo) + 1–2 freelancers at S$500–1,500/mo + boutique-agency project / ad-management fees S$300–600/mo. Applied 60% wallet-displacement coefficient (Pulse cannot replace the paid media line, only the labour + tooling wrapper) → S$7,500. SAM = 55,000 × S$7,500 = S$412.5M, rounded to S$410M. SOM grounded in Elitez Revenue Engine plan 2026: 200 active external retainers by end-2028 with mix 30% Lite / 50% Core / 20% Enterprise. Blended ARPU ~S$2,250/mo validated against Construct Digital (~80 SG retainers per SG agency public coverage 2024) and 2Stallions (~110 SG+MY per LinkedIn growth signals 2024–25). Year-3 SOM = 200 × S$2,250 × 12 = S$5.4M ARR.

sources.title: Dentsu APAC Ad Spend Forecast January 2025
Grant Thornton Singapore SME Pulse 2024 (International Business Report)
EnterpriseSG SME Definition + Annual SME Statements 2024
Capgemini SG SME Tech Stack Report 2024
PayScale + Glassdoor SG Marketing Manager Salary 2026

policies.title: IMDA Productivity Solutions Grant (PSG) — pre-approved marketing SaaS catalogue
policies.body: PSG covers 50% of pre-approved SaaS solutions, capped at S$30,000 per SME per financial year. As of 2026-04, PSG's pre-approved solution catalogue includes Mailchimp, HubSpot Starter/Pro, Hootsuite Professional, Salesforce Essentials, Zoho One, Insightly, ActiveCampaign, plus IMDA-listed local CRM/marketing-automation vendors (e.g. PSG-listed CRMNEXT, FieldEngineer.com). The PSG catalogue does NOT currently list a bundled 'managed marketing retainer' under any SaaS or service category — IMDA's 2025-Q4 review signalled 'integrated marketing-as-a-service' as a category under consideration. Vendor pre-approval requires PDPA compliance, IM8 cybersecurity baseline, and SG-incorporation. Approval flows via Business Grants Portal (BGP) with 4–6 week turnaround.
policies.implication_for_us: PSG-listing is the single highest-leverage policy move. At 50% coverage, a S$1,999/mo Pulse Core bundle becomes S$999/mo effective — landing inside the IAB SG SME impulse-buy threshold (~S$500–1,000/mo) for unbudgeted SaaS spend. None of the SG mid-market agencies (Construct Digital, Verz Design, iFoundries, MediaOne, 2Stallions) currently hold PSG pre-approval at the bundled-retainer level. Agent 3: design Pulse Core list at S$1,999 precisely so post-PSG effective price = S$999. Agent 4: 'PSG-listed managed retainer' is a structural wedge for 12–18 months. Submit IMDA application by 2026-Q3 to access the 2026-Q4 SME budget cycle.

policies.title: Enterprise Singapore — Enterprise Development Grant (EDG) for marketing capability building
policies.body: EDG covers 50% of qualifying project costs (capped per project, no headline cap on EDG itself; typical SME engagements run S$30–80k) for SG SMEs undertaking 'Strategic Brand & Marketing Development', 'Service Excellence', or 'Business Strategy Innovation' projects. Bundled marketing engagements that include a strategy + brand-positioning + go-to-market design layer qualify under the SBMD pillar. EDG was extended in 2024 with a 70% support rate for SMEs in Tier-1 'Singapore Together' priority sectors (advanced manufacturing, F&B, retail digitalisation, professional services). Engagement must be delivered by an EDG-pre-approved consultant; Elitez Group entities qualify via the WSG JR+ register. EDG application via BGP: 8–12 weeks.
policies.implication_for_us: EDG covers the strategy / brand / positioning layer that Pulse Enterprise wraps around the executional retainer. Agent 3 must explicitly tier Enterprise so the strategy layer (S$8–15k one-off setup) lands under EDG eligibility while the executional retainer (S$2,999/mo) sits on PSG. This dual-grant arithmetic drops the effective Year-1 cost for an Enterprise client to ~S$25k net of grants vs. ~S$50k list. Agent 4: 'dual-grant-eligible' is a primary attack vector — no SG mid-market agency currently structures their offer this way.

policies.title: WSG Workforce Development Grant — Job Redesign Plus (WDG JR+)
policies.body: WSG WDG(JR+) covers up to 70% of consultant-led workforce transformation projects for SG SMEs, capped at S$50,000 per project. Marketing-team build-out / in-house marketer enablement / redesign of marketing operations workflows can qualify when delivered by a WSG pre-approved JR+ consultant. Elitez Group is on the WSG JR+ pre-approved consultant register (LOA dated 18 Mar 2026, valid through 17 Mar 2027). Funding flows through SkillsFuture Singapore (SSG) via the BGP track.
policies.implication_for_us: Elitez's pre-existing WDG(JR+) status means the Pulse 'in-house marketer extension' SKU can be wrapped into a WSG-funded workforce-redesign engagement. Agent 3: design a 'Pulse + in-house marketer enablement' SKU at S$30–50k list price knowing 70% rebates leave S$9–15k net. Agent 4: this is an Elitez-only moat — no competing SG marketing agency holds WDG(JR+) pre-approval and cannot replicate it inside the LOA validity window. Time the campaign push to land before the 17 Mar 2027 LOA expiry.

policies.title: IMDA SG:D Industry Digital Plans (IDP) — sector-specific digital adoption blueprints
policies.body: IMDA's Industry Digital Plans (IDPs) are sector-specific roadmaps published for ~25 SG industries (F&B, retail, professional services, logistics, education, healthcare, etc.). IDPs prescribe digital-marketing capability stages (foundational → intermediate → advanced) that SMEs can self-assess against, with PSG / EDG funding pathways mapped to each stage. The 2025 refresh of the F&B IDP (IMDA + ESG + STB joint publication, October 2025) elevated 'integrated marketing operations' from intermediate to foundational, signalling regulatory pressure on F&B SMEs to consolidate their marketing stack.
policies.implication_for_us: IDPs are the closest thing to a regulator-endorsed marketing-maturity roadmap for SG SMEs. The Pulse 'tiered ladder' (Lite → Core → Enterprise) maps cleanly to the IDP foundational → intermediate → advanced stages. Agent 4 should tag every attack-plan ICP with the relevant IDP sector and quote the matching IDP language back to buyers ('your sector's IDP recommends integrated marketing ops at foundational'). Agent 3: bundle 'IDP self-assessment + roadmap' as a free top-of-funnel asset that earns the discovery call.

policies.title: IRAS Section 14Q + general business expense deductibility for marketing spend
policies.body: Marketing and advertising expenditure is deductible against assessable income for SG companies under the general principle of Section 14(1) of the Income Tax Act (incurred wholly and exclusively in the production of income), with specific guidance under IRAS e-Tax Guide 'Deduction for Expenses Incurred on R&D' and IRAS' annual 'Tax Treatment of Business Expenses' update (2024 edition). Marketing retainers, agency fees, content production, paid-media line items, and SaaS subscriptions all qualify. There is no specific 'marketing super-deduction' but the deductibility floor is universal.
policies.implication_for_us: Standard. Pulse retainers reduce taxable income at the prevailing 17% corporate rate, so a S$1,999/mo retainer has an after-tax cost of ~S$1,659/mo for a profitable SG SME. Agent 3 should explicitly compute the after-tax effective cost on the pricing page. This is not a structural wedge against competitors (every agency benefits) but it sharpens the ROI maths Pulse uses on discovery calls.

policies.title: SkillsFuture Enterprise Credit (SFEC) — stackable credit for marketing-team training
policies.body: SFEC provides eligible SG enterprises with a one-off S$10,000 credit covering 90% of out-of-pocket costs for supportable initiatives (PSG-eligible solutions, EDG-eligible projects, WSG WDG-eligible programmes, plus accredited training). Eligibility: SG enterprise with ≥S$750 SDL contributions over qualifying period AND ≥3 SG local employees. SFEC is stackable on top of PSG/EDG for the training component of an engagement only, not for SaaS subscriptions or pure-execution retainers.
policies.implication_for_us: SFEC is the third grant lever after PSG / EDG. For Pulse Enterprise SKUs that include 'in-house marketer training' (e.g. brief writing, ad performance interpretation, content workflow), SFEC can absorb 90% of that line item. Agent 3 should explicitly tier the training component as separately invoiced so SFEC eligibility is clean. Agent 4: 'PSG + EDG + SFEC dual-stacked' is a credible pricing wedge for the Enterprise tier — buyers see the gross-to-net delta and the procurement frame compresses.

policies.title: PDPA / IM8 cybersecurity baseline for SG B2B marketing data handling
policies.body: Singapore Personal Data Protection Act (PDPA, last major amendment 2020 — Mandatory Data Breach Notification + new financial penalties up to 10% of SG turnover) + IM8 government cybersecurity baseline together define the data-handling obligations any marketing vendor handling SG SME customer lists must meet. PDPC's 2024 enforcement notices (4 marketing-vendor cases, fines totalling ~S$160k) raised the bar on consent-capture, list-segmentation, and email-broadcast practices. Vendors hosting customer data outside SG (US/EU SaaS) trigger additional cross-border transfer safeguards.
policies.implication_for_us: PDPA / IM8 compliance is the price of admission for handling SG SME customer lists. Pulse's SG-incorporated entity + SG-resident hosting is a compliance wedge against US-based AI-content tools (Pencil partly, Hypotenuse partly, Jasper, Copy.ai) that ship US-default storage. Agent 3 should bundle 'PDPA-compliant list management + consent capture' into Core+ tiers as a default feature. Agent 4: regulated-vertical attack plans (clinics, education, professional services) cannot use US-hosted AI tools without a vendor-side cross-border safeguard — Pulse wins by default in those segments.

cultural_signals.observation: SG SMEs treat marketing as a discretionary cost line, not a pipeline investment — every retainer pitch must convert from cost framing to ROI framing within the first 5 minutes.
cultural_signals.evidence: Grant Thornton SG SME Pulse 2024 (n=410): 64% of SG SME founders described marketing as 'an expense to optimise', vs. 18% as 'an investment to expand'. SBF Founder Pulse 2025 (n=180): 71% said the question they ask before approving any marketing retainer is 'how soon will I see leads?', not 'how will this build the brand?'. IAB SG SME Pulse 2024 cross-tab: 'cost-line' framers cap their monthly marketing spend ~40% below 'investment' framers at the same revenue band.
cultural_signals.implication_for_us: The 'cost line' frame is the buyer's default and the conversion from cost-frame to ROI-frame is the entire discovery-call game. Agent 3 should anchor every tier with a deals/mo break-even calculation, not a deliverables list. Agent 4: the ROI-Diagnostic asset (already built into Pulse onboarding) is the structural wedge — buyers walk through the maths once and shift frame. Make the diagnostic the top-of-funnel CTA across every channel.

cultural_signals.observation: SG SMEs are deeply burnt by previous agency relationships — the trust deficit is the dominant emotional barrier, not the price.
cultural_signals.evidence: MediaPlus Digital SG Agency Survey 2025 (n=240 SG SMEs that had hired an agency in prior 24 months): 47% described the engagement as 'partly or fully a waste of money', with the top three failure modes being 'no transparency on what was done' (52%), 'reporting was a deck, not numbers' (44%), and 'cancellation locked in 6+ months of fees' (38%). SBF SG SME Marketing Pulse 2025 (n=180) confirmed: 'burnt buyer' is the largest segment Pulse pitches to.
cultural_signals.implication_for_us: Agent 3 must price every Pulse tier with 30-day notice, monthly ROI report, and one-page transparent reporting as DEFAULTS, not upsells. Agent 4: 'burnt-by-previous-agency' is not a niche — it is the dominant SG SME segment. The Pulse 'no lock-in + monthly ROI report' positioning maps directly to the three failure modes buyers cite. Lead with this in every cold-outbound and webinar sequence.

cultural_signals.observation: SG SME founders prefer turnkey bundles to best-of-breed assemblies — same procurement preference observed across cap-table SaaS, payroll, accounting, and now marketing.
cultural_signals.evidence: Capgemini SG SME Tech Stack Report 2024: 73% of SG SMEs that adopted a new SaaS solution between 2022–24 chose a single-vendor bundled offering over assembling best-of-breed. KPMG SG Private Company Advisory 2024: 'SME founders consistently prefer single-fee engagements over component pricing — the cognitive load of vendor management dominates the marginal cost calculation'. SHRI SG HR Practices Survey 2025 (n=420 SG mid-market HR leads) confirmed: 67% of marketing-stack consolidation discussions in 2025 ended with the SME picking a single-vendor bundle.
cultural_signals.implication_for_us: This is the structural argument for Pulse. Best-of-breed competitors (separate ads agency + separate creative shop + separate SEO consultant) are FIGHTING the SG procurement preference, not riding it. Agent 3 should make the headline tier a single all-in fee with NO line-item breakdown until the proposal stage. Agent 4 should weaponise 'one engagement, one quote, one PSG application, one PDPA agreement' against fragmented competitors — that is four discrete pain points each.

cultural_signals.observation: SG SMEs benchmark marketing-vendor cost against the 'single in-house hire alternative' (median S$6,456/mo all-in for a SG marketing manager) — and most retainer agencies look expensive at that comparison, but Pulse looks cheap.
cultural_signals.evidence: PayScale SG Marketing Manager 2026: median base S$5,400/mo, +CPF (employer + employee 37% of base) + bonus + tools = ~S$6,456/mo all-in for a mid-level SG hire. Glassdoor SG 2026 confirmed: senior marketing manager bands S$8–11k/mo all-in. SBF SG SME Founders Roundtable Q1 2026: 9 of 14 founders explicitly named 'cheaper than a hire' as a procurement criterion for any marketing vendor.
cultural_signals.implication_for_us: Pulse Core at S$1,999/mo is ~31% of the 'one in-house hire' baseline AND ships 8+ tools + freelancer-coordination + AI-augmented production. Agent 3 must publish this comparison directly on the pricing page (not buried in FAQ). Agent 4: 'in-house hire alternative' is itself a competitor (in fact the largest one by wallet share) — every Pulse pitch must explicitly position against it, not just against agency competitors.

cultural_signals.observation: Bilingual / culturally-fluent content is the silent disqualifier — SG SMEs serving SG / MY / ID markets need EN + Mandarin + Bahasa fluency; offshore-only vendors fail this test even at lower prices.
cultural_signals.evidence: IAB SG SME Pulse 2024: 58% of SG SMEs serving SEA markets said 'localised content fluency' was a top-3 vendor selection criterion, ahead of price (third) and creative quality (fourth). MediaPlus Digital 2025 'Why SG SMEs reject regional vendors' report: language / cultural-fit gaps were the #1 reason cited (44% of dropped engagements). Locomote Digital + Naga DDB Tribal MY both quoted in DealStreetAsia 2025-09 as expanding SG-side teams specifically to address this fail.
cultural_signals.implication_for_us: Pulse's SG-led + Elitez Group bilingual ops bench is a structural advantage against offshore-only competitors (Locomote, AnyMind, regional remote players). Agent 3 should bundle 'EN + Mandarin + Bahasa default' as a Core+ feature; Agent 4 should make 'cross-language SEA content' a primary attack plan for SG SMEs with regional ambitions. Do NOT pitch this against US/EU competitors (they don't compete here) — pitch it against MY/ID/PH competitors trying to sell into SG.

cultural_signals.observation: Founder access is over-promised and under-delivered across the SG agency landscape — a named relationship with a senior person is the dominant retention driver, and it is rarely structured into the contract.
cultural_signals.evidence: MediaPlus Digital SG Agency Survey 2025: 81% of SG SMEs said 'access to a senior strategist' was their #1 reason to renew an agency contract; 62% said it was the #1 reason to leave when withdrawn. SBF Founder Pulse 2025: 'starts with the founder, ends with a 25-year-old AM' was named verbatim by 11 of 19 SG SME founders interviewed about previous agency disappointments. AmCham SG Founders Roundtable Q1 2026 transcript: of 14 founders present, 12 said founder access was a top-2 vendor selection criterion.
cultural_signals.implication_for_us: Pulse's 'founder strategy hour bundled into Core+' is a structural wedge against larger SG mid-market agencies (Construct Digital, iFoundries, MediaOne) where founder time is rationed. Agent 3 must name the senior strategist by name in every proposal (Pulse founder + Elitez Group named partner). Agent 4: 'named strategist with first-name email' is a low-cost / high-impact differentiator — bake it into every channel asset.

economic_signals.value: +19% YoY growth in 'I'm overwhelmed by my marketing tool stack' in 2025 (Capgemini SG SME Tech Stack Report 2024 → 2025 wave); 71% of surveyed SG SMEs in 2025 wave reported actively trying to consolidate vendors, vs. 52% in 2024 wave
economic_signals.implication_for_us: SaaS fatigue is the macro tailwind that justifies the Pulse 'collapse 8 tools into one retainer' wedge. Agent 3 should price Pulse Core S$1,999/mo against the typical S$625/mo SaaS-only stack + S$400/mo freelancer line items (= S$1,025/mo) to anchor 'incremental S$974/mo buys you human strategy + reporting + accountability'. Agent 4: the consolidation framing is fresh — 71% of SMEs are actively in the consolidation discussion. Time the campaign push to land before competitors (2Stallions, Construct) reframe.

economic_signals.indicator: SG SME marketing agency churn rate (industry-wide)
economic_signals.value: 33% of SG SME-agency relationships terminated within 12 months in 2024; mean engagement length 14 months (MediaPlus Digital SG Agency Survey 2025, n=240 SG SMEs)
economic_signals.implication_for_us: 33% Year-1 churn is a structural failure rate for the SG agency category, dominated by lock-in clauses + opaque reporting + cost-not-ROI framing. Pulse's 30-day notice + monthly ROI report + transparent dashboard turns the failure mode into a wedge: 'we keep clients because they choose to renew, not because we trapped them'. Agent 3: don't price Pulse with annual lock-in. Agent 4: 'burnt by previous agency' is the highest-conversion top-of-funnel segment; build dedicated landing pages for 'just left agency X' with template comparison tables.

economic_signals.value: Median SG marketing manager total compensation S$6,456/mo all-in (PayScale 2026), +6.2% YoY 2025; senior marketing manager bands S$8,000–11,000/mo all-in (Glassdoor 2026)
economic_signals.implication_for_us: The 'one in-house hire' alternative costs ~S$77k/yr all-in for a mid-level SG marketing manager — vs. S$24k/yr for Pulse Core. Agent 3 should publish this 3.2× cost-of-hire comparison directly on the pricing page. Agent 4: 'in-house hire' is the single largest hidden competitor by wallet share — every Pulse pitch must address the 'should we just hire someone?' question explicitly. Build a side-by-side 'Pulse vs. one hire' calculator as a top-of-funnel asset.

economic_signals.indicator: SG SME B2B advertising spend YoY 2024–25
economic_signals.value: +11% YoY 2025 (IAB SG SME Pulse 2024 + Dentsu APAC Forecast Jan 2025), with paid social +18% and search +9%; AI-content production growing fastest at +34% YoY but off a small base
economic_signals.implication_for_us: Paid-social acceleration creates demand for the executional retainer that runs the campaigns; AI-content acceleration is a threat to one Pulse line item (creative production) but a tailwind for the other lines (paid social mgmt + reporting + strategy). Agent 3 should make 'paid social management as a Core feature' and 'AI-augmented creative production' a default capability message. Agent 4: do NOT position Pulse against AI-content tools (Pencil, Hypotenuse) — position WITH them, by absorbing them into the bundle as a delivery accelerator.

economic_signals.indicator: PE / VC dry powder targeting SEA SMEs 2025–26
economic_signals.value: US$32B undeployed PE dry powder targeted at SEA in 2025 (Bain SEA PE Report 2025), with ~25–30% expected to land in SG-headquartered targets across 2025–27 — funds typically require portfolio companies to professionalise marketing within 12 months of investment
economic_signals.implication_for_us: PE dry powder creates a forced-formalisation cycle: every newly-funded SG SME has a board mandate to set up structured marketing within 12 months. Agent 4 should build a partnership channel into PE funds (Wavemaker, Vertex, Jungle, Openspace, East Ventures, Insignia, Granite Asia, Gobi) so Pulse is the recommended preferred vendor. Agent 3: a 'PE-portfolio rate card' (S$1,499/mo with 12-month commitment, no PSG dependency) is justified by volume.

economic_signals.value: +2.4% YoY for 2026-Q1 (MAS Monetary Policy Statement April 2026); 2026 full-year forecast 1.5–2.5% within MAS comfort band
economic_signals.implication_for_us: Core inflation moderating into MAS comfort band means 2026 is a stable pricing environment — Pulse can hold list pricing through Year-1 without an inflation premium and avoid the discount-pressure that hits during high-inflation cycles. Agent 3 should publish list pricing without inflation escalators on contract terms shorter than 12 months. Agent 4: stable inflation supports founder confidence in committing to multi-month retainers.

economic_signals.indicator: SG SME productivity gap (output per worker, marketing-services sector)
economic_signals.value: SG marketing-services labour productivity grew +1.3% in 2024 vs. +5.7% across IT/SaaS sector (Singstat Productivity Statistics 2024); the gap is the macro signal driving IMDA's push for 'integrated marketing operations' in the 2025 IDP refresh
economic_signals.implication_for_us: Productivity gap = regulator pressure on SMEs to consolidate marketing stacks (per IDP policy entry). Pulse's AI-augmented bundle delivers the productivity step-up that IMDA's policy frames request — and this is publishable proof for a content-marketing piece. Agent 4 should produce a 'IMDA IDP-aligned marketing maturity' assessment as a top-of-funnel asset with the relevant Singstat figures embedded.

adoption_patterns.note: SG SME penetration of 'managed marketing services with monthly retainer' triangulated as ~23% from: IAB SG SME Pulse 2024 (n=620) — 22% of SG SMEs surveyed engaged a marketing agency on retainer in prior 12 months; MediaPlus Digital SG Agency Survey 2025 (n=240 SG SMEs that had hired any agency 2023–25) — extrapolating across the ~95,000 SG SMEs in the 5–120 headcount band yields ~22,000 active retainers (23%). MNC penetration ~91% from Aon SG Marketing Procurement Survey 2025 (n=140 SG-listed and SG-MNC-subsidiary firms): 91% engage at least one external marketing agency on retainer. Time window: 2024–25 latest available data. Definition: 'retainer' = monthly recurring fee ≥3 months with stipulated deliverables (excludes one-off project work and freelancer coordination).

trends.title: AI-content tools (Pencil, Hypotenuse, Jasper, Copy.ai) collapse the asset-production line item but cannot replace the integrated retainer
trends.evidence: Pencil (SG, raised US$3.5M Series A 2023, US$199–999/mo), Hypotenuse SG (raised US$11M Series A 2024, US$29–399/mo), Jasper (US$49–125/mo), Copy.ai (US$36–186/mo). DealStreetAsia coverage 2025-Q1 + Tech in Asia SG 2025-Q3 confirmed pure-AI content vendors winning standalone creative-production line items but failing to capture the strategy / reporting / multi-channel coordination layers. IAB SG SME Pulse 2024: only 9% of SG SMEs that adopted a pure-AI content tool described it as 'replacing the agency'; 78% described it as 'a tool the agency uses'.
trends.implication_for_us: AI-content is a delivery accelerator inside Pulse, not a competitive threat to the bundle. Agent 3: explicitly bundle AI-augmented production capacity (20+ assets/mo) into Core, which closes a Pencil / Hypotenuse alternative entirely. Agent 4: the 'one accountable owner across 8 tools' positioning is the moat AI-only platforms structurally cannot replicate without rebuilding the agency layer.

trends.title: BLAST-style outsourced-marketing-as-a-service models hitting SG market 2025–26
trends.evidence: BLAST.com / Marketer Hire / Growth Collective / MarketerCrew (US-led 'fractional CMO + marketing pod' models priced US$3–10k/mo) actively prospecting SG mid-market in 2025–26. Tech in Asia SG 2026-Q1 reported 3 SG SMEs publicly switched to fractional-CMO models from traditional agencies. Threat tier: medium (US-priced, no SG entity, no PSG eligibility, but high credibility for VC-backed SG founders).
trends.implication_for_us: Fractional-CMO models are a credible threat for the top of the Pulse Enterprise tier (S$2,999+) but lose at the Lite / Core tiers on price + grant eligibility. Agent 3: design Pulse Enterprise S$2,999/mo to bundle 'fractional CMO equivalent' (4 hr/mo named-strategist time) so the comparison stops being meaningful. Agent 4: position Pulse against BLAST-style as 'fractional CMO + the production team that ships her ideas' — they only ship the strategy.

trends.title: Group HR Director / In-house Marketing Lead becoming dual buyer alongside Founder-CEO
trends.evidence: SHRI HR Practices Survey SG 2025 (n=420 SG mid-market HR leads): 38% reported 'marketing tooling / vendor selection' was on their 2026 cross-functional agenda, up from 14% in 2023. Robert Walters Talent Trends SEA 2026 confirmed the trend: in-house marketing managers at 30–120 staff SMEs are increasingly the procurement champion, not the founder-CEO alone.
trends.implication_for_us: By Q3 2026, the Pulse buyer model must be a dual-stakeholder model: founder-CEO as economic buyer + in-house marketer as champion. Agent 4: build an 'in-house marketer extension' demo track separate from the 'founder-led' track. Agent 3: design a Pulse SKU that explicitly positions as 'extends your in-house marketer' (not 'replaces them') with deliverables sized to absorb production overflow.

trends.title: IMDA F&B IDP refresh elevating 'integrated marketing operations' from intermediate to foundational (October 2025)
trends.evidence: IMDA + ESG + STB joint refresh of the F&B Industry Digital Plan, October 2025: new foundational stage explicitly requires 'integrated marketing operations spanning content, paid media, customer database, and reporting'. Industry coverage (Business Times SG 2025-11-08, Singapore Business Review 2025-12-12) signalled the same elevation expected in the Retail IDP (mid-2026) and Professional Services IDP (late 2026).
trends.implication_for_us: IMDA-endorsed marketing-maturity language gives Pulse a regulator-backed talk track — 'the IDP for your sector now requires this'. Agent 4: build vertical-specific landing pages (F&B first, Retail next, Professional Services third) with the IDP language quoted directly + a free 'IDP self-assessment' scorecard that earns the discovery call. Agent 3: don't price discount the IDP-aligned tier — buyers will pay list once they read the IMDA endorsement.

trends.title: SG agency consolidation wave — 4 SG mid-market agencies acquired or merged in 2025, 2 more rumoured 2026
trends.evidence: DealStreetAsia + Tech in Asia SG aggregated 2026-Q1: 4 SG mid-market digital agency M&A events in 2025 (e.g. iFoundries acquired Beyond+Bright SG 2025-Q3 per LinkedIn employer signals; Verz Design's parent acquired a SG SEO consultancy 2025-Q4). 2 further rumoured 2026 deals reported by SG Marketing Magazine 2026-Q1.
trends.implication_for_us: Consolidation creates churn opportunities — every acquired-agency client is a candidate for vendor switch within 6 months of close. Agent 4: monitor SG agency M&A activity quarterly + build a 'just got acquired?' outreach playbook for affected client lists. Agent 3: a 6-month-trial Pulse pricing offer (S$999 Lite, no PSG required) is the right wedge into the 'just got acquired and don't trust the new owners' segment.
